findings.message: N+1 query pattern: the handler first selects all item ids, then issues a separate `session.get(Item, iid)` per id inside the loop. For N items this executes N+1 round-trips to the database, which scales linearly with table size on an unbounded input and will exhaust connection time / pool capacity under load. This is a clear regression against any 'list endpoint latency' SLO.
findings.suggestion: Fetch the names in a single query, e.g. `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`. If you must keep the two-step shape, replace the per-id `session.get` with one `select(Item).where(Item.id.in_(item_ids))` (or use `selectinload`) so the whole result set is materialized in one round-trip.

findings.message: The function is annotated `-> list[str]` and declares `response_model=list[str]`, but never returns `names`. At runtime it returns `None`, which FastAPI will then fail to serialize against the response model — a correctness bug that also masks the performance issue (the loop's work is discarded).
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` at the end of the function (and prefer the single-query form above).

findings.message: `from sqlalchemy import select` is performed inside the request handler. The import is cached after the first call so the cost is negligible, but moving it to module scope keeps the hot path allocation-free and matches the style used elsewhere in the package (e.g. `db/repository.py`).
findings.suggestion: Hoist `from sqlalchemy import select` to the top of the module.
